ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a whole wheat bread entry; the jam topping component is not separately coded — closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E covers wholemeal bread; jam spread component is not separately represented in FoodEx2 at this level.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No sufficiently specific SNOMED CT code identified for wholemeal bread with jam as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is two slices of wholemeal bread (approximately 80 g total) each spread with approximately 10 g of fruit jam.

nutrient_source.title: Wholemeal bread with jam — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F05020139 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per-100g values are taken from this primary source.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values — wholemeal/whole wheat bread
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 69 is consistent with published values for wholemeal bread in the University of Sydney GI Database (range 60–74 for wholemeal/whole wheat bread). The jam component raises the overall sugar load but does not substantially alter the bread-dominant GI. GL per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (69 × 35.4 g carbohydrate in 80 g serving) / 100 ≈ 24.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 285 mg/100 g and sugar 12.45 g/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 285 mg/100 g contributes meaningfully to daily sodium intake; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one slice and choose a reduced-sugar or no-added-sugar jam to avoid the compounding effect of high sugar on vascular tone.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (69), sugar 12.45 g/100 g, glycemic load ~24 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: The medium GI of 69 combined with a glycemic load of approximately 24 per standard serving makes this food a moderate glycaemic challenge; people with type 2 diabetes should limit to one slice, pair with a protein source such as a boiled egg, and replace fruit jam with a no-added-sugar spread to reduce postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Wholegrain fibre 4.65 g/100 g, low fat 3.31 g/100 g, moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The wholemeal base provides 4.65 g dietary fibre per 100 g which supports LDL cholesterol reduction; however, sodium at 285 mg/100 g and added sugar from jam should be moderated — choose a low-sodium wholemeal bread and a no-added-sugar jam to optimise cardiovascular benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sugar 12.45 g/100 g from jam — potential pro-inflammatory load
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Sugar at 12.45 g/100 g from concentrated fruit jam may contribute to low-grade inflammation; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis are advised to replace jam with anti-inflammatory toppings such as avocado or walnut butter, or use fresh berries to obtain fruit flavour with antioxidant benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wholemeal bread with jam is a common breakfast and snack item consisting of sliced wholemeal (whole wheat) bread spread with fruit jam. The wholemeal bread is made from whole wheat flour that retains the bran and germ, giving it a denser texture, nuttier flavour, and higher fibre content compared to white bread. Fruit jam — typically strawberry, raspberry, or mixed fruit — provides sweetness and a soft, glossy spread. Per 100 g the dish delivers 242 kcal, 9.77 g protein, 3.31 g fat, 44.22 g carbohydrate, 12.45 g sugar, 4.65 g dietary fibre, 285 mg sodium, 142 mg calcium, and 4.58 mg iron. The medium glycemic index of 69 and glycemic load of approximately 24 per 80 g serving indicate a moderate impact on blood glucose. The high fibre content from wholemeal flour supports digestive health and LDL cholesterol reduction, but the concentrated sugar from jam raises concern for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or inflammatory conditions. This food is widely consumed in Singapore as a quick breakfast, often paired with tea or coffee. It is suitable for vegetarians and vegans and is halal-friendly. Clinically, the combination of wholegrain fibre and moderate protein makes it a reasonable energy source, but the jam-derived sugar load should be moderated for metabolically vulnerable individuals.

confidence_reason: All core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with a review_status of reviewed and confidence 0.95. The GI value of 69 is consistent with published University of Sydney GI Database values for wholemeal bread. No estimation or interpolation was required for core macros.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific HPB composite entry (wholemeal bread with jam, F05020139) are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026-04-16. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (69 × 35.4 g available carbohydrate in an 80 g serving) / 100 ≈ 24.4. The food is classified as a Western/International item widely consumed in Singapore; multilingual names are provided in Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi in accordance with Singaporean multilingual naming requirements.
